text: The hysteroscope is introduced through the adequately dilated cervical os and advanced to the uterine fundus. Bilateral osteo are visualized, with no septum or cavity abnormalities observed. Upon retracting the hysteroscope to the lower uterine segment, a concave defect is visible on the anterior wall, indicating the cesarean scar defect.

The surgeon uses a hysteroscopic resectoscope to denude the concave defect in the lower uterine segment. After completing the denudation, the hysteroscope is removed, and a uterine manipulator is inserted into the cavity.

The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, visually confirming the uteroperitoneal fistulas tract and dense adhesions in the abdomen. Using cold laparoscopic scissors, the omental adhesions are carefully removed, with particular attention to avoid the bladder due to the severity of the adhesions.

The surgeon begins dissection laterally near the obliterated umbilical vessels using a CO-II laser to incise the peritoneum, creating new spaces on both sides. The dissection is extended through to the vesicocervical space, combining the newly created bilateral spaces.

The surgeon uses a combination of CO-II laser and blunt dissection to mobilize the bladder. The CO-II laser provides precise and superficial dissection, minimizing thermal spread and potential injury to the bladder. The dissection is directed inferiorly to create adequate space for the upcoming excision and repair of the niche.
The surgeon carefully dissects the dense fibrotic tissue layer by layer, mapping out the fistula tract until reaching the defect at the anterior uterine wall.

The surgeon removes the uterine manipulator and introduces the hysteroscope transvaginally to the lower uterine segment, advancing it into the niche under direct laparoscopic visualization. The thinned myometrium is visible due to the extrusion of the hysteroscope and illumination, along with pressure from the hysteroscopic saline infusion, facilitating laparoscopic mapping and excision of the defect.

The surgeon performs a wide laparoscopic excision of the peripheral tissue of the niche, allowing the hysteroscope to penetrate into the abdominal cavity. The excised fistula tissue is sent for pathological examination. The defect is repaired in two layers using zero V-lock suture. Fresh bleeding at the edges is controlled with desiccation, achieving good hemostasis, and the re-approximated tissue is confirmed to be intact.
